text.title: Django Placement Examples and Antipatterns
text.summary: Correct service, utils, and integration patterns with code examples; three critical placement antipatterns.

example.code:
# services/orders.py — DB writes with transaction from django.db import transaction
@transaction.atomic def create_order(user, items, *, shipping_address): order = Order.objects.create( user=user, shipping_address=shipping_address, status=OrderStatus.PENDING, ) for item in items: inventory_services.reserve_stock(item['product_id'], item['quantity']) order.items.create(**item) order.calculate_total() return order
example.note: Service owns the transaction boundary and calls other services. No HTTP context, no request object.
Pure function: same input always produces same output. Trivial to unit test.

antipattern.description: Database query in utils/ (e.g., User.objects.filter() inside a utils function).
antipattern.reason: utils/ functions have no DB dependency by convention. Adding ORM calls makes them non-pure, breaks unit testability, and blurs the service/util boundary. Move to services/.

antipattern.description: Business logic in views/ (conditional rules, calculations, multi-step DB operations directly in the view method).
antipattern.reason: Business logic in views is untestable without an HTTP context. The view becomes a 200-line method that is impossible to reuse or test in isolation. Delegate to services/.

antipattern.description: Circular imports between apps (apps/orders/services.py importing from apps/users/services.py when users already imports orders).
antipattern.reason: Circular imports cause ImportError at startup. Use Django signals, Celery tasks, or pass data by value to break cycles. Never import circularly — restructure the dependency.
